perturbation.original_text: 4. Such Confidential Information shall also include but shall not be limited to: a) information disclosed by the Disclosing Party in writing marked as confidential at the time of disclosure; b) information disclosed by the Disclosing Party orally which is slated to be confidential at the time of disclosure; c) information disclosed in any other manner is designated in writing as Confidential Information at the time of disclosure; or d) notwithstanding sub-clauses a,b & c of this definition, any information whose nature makes it obvious that it is confidential.
perturbation.changed_text: 4. Such Confidential Information shall also include but shall not be limited to: a) information disclosed by the Disclosing Party in writing marked as confidential at the time of disclosure; b) information disclosed by the Disclosing Party orally which is slated to be confidential at the time of disclosure; c) information disclosed in any other manner is designated in writing as Confidential Information at the time of disclosure; or d) notwithstanding sub-clauses a,b & c of this definition, any information whose nature makes it ambiguous whether it is confidential.
perturbation.explanation: By replacing 'obvious that it is confidential' with 'ambiguous whether it is confidential', the contract now implies that even if the confidential nature of information is unclear, it might still be considered confidential. This creates uncertainty and contradicts standard legal interpretations where a reasonable person would understand the confidential nature of the data.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 43A deals with compensation for failure to protect sensitive personal data. If the contract defines confidential information so ambiguously that it includes data that a reasonable person wouldn't consider confidential, it weakens the protections required under Section 43A. It could be argued that the Receiving Party has no reasonable way to determine what data falls under the protection of the NDA, thus potentially reducing liability for failing to protect ambiguously defined data.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract text replaces 'obvious that it is confidential' with 'ambiguous whether it is confidential.' This introduces uncertainty and contradicts standard legal interpretations. The LLM explanation notes that this ambiguity weakens the protections required under Section 43A. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is a 'Document not Found' error and Snippet 2 is empty, the Perturbation Explanation clearly indicates a deviation from standard legal practice where confidentiality is reasonably understood. Therefore, the modification creates a legal contradiction by potentially reducing liability for failing to protect ambiguously defined data.

perturbation.original_text: 3. At any time upon request from the Disclosing Party or upon the conclusion of the Purpose or expiry of this Agreement, the Receiving Party, at its own cost, will return or procure the return, promptly and in any event within 14 days of receipt of such request, of each and every copy of Confidential Information given by the Disclosing Party, and satisfy the Disclosing Party that it no longer holds any further Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: 3. At any time upon request from the Disclosing Party or upon the conclusion of the Purpose or expiry of this Agreement, the Receiving Party, at its own cost, will return or procure the return, promptly and in any event within 14 days of receipt of such request, of each and every copy of Confidential Information given by the Disclosing Party, but is not required to assure or prove to the Disclosing Party that it no longer holds any further Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the obligation for the Receiving Party to 'satisfy the Disclosing Party that it no longer holds any further Confidential Information,' the contract weakens the enforcement mechanism for protecting confidential data. This contradicts the implicit duty of good faith and fair dealing that is generally expected in contractual relationships under Indian law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Sections 36-42 of the Specific Relief Act concern injunctions and specific performance of contracts. By removing the obligation to 'satisfy' the Disclosing Party, it becomes more difficult for the Disclosing Party to obtain an injunction compelling the Receiving Party to return or destroy confidential information, as they cannot easily prove that the Receiving Party is still in possession of the data. This undermines the effectiveness of the remedies available under the Act.
perturbation.location: V. Copying and Return of Furnished Instruments: 3

perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the Receiving Party's obligation to 'satisfy the Disclosing Party that it no longer holds any further Confidential Information'. This directly weakens the Disclosing Party's ability to enforce the confidentiality agreement and ensure the return or destruction of confidential information. The Law Explanation suggests this could impact the ability to obtain an injunction. While the snippets provided do not explicitly state this, the removal of the 'satisfy' obligation fundamentally alters the level of assurance and verification required, creating a situation where the Disclosing Party has significantly less recourse, therefore creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: VIII. Intellectual Property Rights: All Confidential Information disclosed herein shall remain the sole property of the Disclosing Party and the Receiving Party shall obtain no right thereto of any kind by reason of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: VIII. Intellectual Property Rights: All Confidential Information disclosed herein shall remain the sole property of the Disclosing Party unless the receiving party adds substantial modification or creativity to it, in which case, the receiving party will obtain derivative rights and shared ownership.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying this clause to allow the Receiving Party to gain derivative rights and shared ownership of Confidential Information based on 'substantial modification or creativity' directly contradicts the principle that the Disclosing Party retains sole ownership. This introduces ambiguity and potential disputes over IP ownership, going against typical NDA provisions.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 17 of the Copyright Act establishes the owner of copyright. This modification suggests that the Receiving Party could gain copyright ownership of the Disclosing Party's confidential information through 'substantial modification,' potentially conflicting with the Disclosing Party's original ownership rights and the general intent of copyright law to protect the original creator.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: This page is better viewed with a JavaScript enabled browser. Document not Found Document not found The document you were looking for does not exist. Click here to close this window, or click here to go back.
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text introduces a contradiction regarding intellectual property rights. The original text clearly states that the Disclosing Party retains sole ownership of Confidential Information. The changed text allows the Receiving Party to gain derivative rights and shared ownership if they add 'substantial modification or creativity.' The Law Explanation states that Section 17 of the Copyright Act establishes the owner of copyright, and this change could conflict with the Disclosing Party's original ownership rights. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is a 'Document not found' error and Snippet 2 is empty, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation clearly highlight the contradiction related to the Disclosing Party potentially losing their original ownership rights based on copyright law, making the change non-compliant and creating a legal contradiction. The misaligned terminology shifts the ownership rights in a way that undermines the original intent of protecting the Disclosing Party's intellectual property.
